concepts.lesson: Sound is a form of energy that creates a hearing sensation[cite: 4, 502]. It is produced when objects vibrate, which means they move rapidly back and forth[cite: 43, 541]. This vibration can be started by striking, plucking, rubbing, or blowing on various objects[cite: 41, 539].
concepts.real_example: The human voice is created by the vibration of vocal cords[cite: 44, 542].
key_points: Sound is a form of energy[cite: 4, 502].
Vibrating objects produce sound[cite: 51, 549].
Vibration is rapid to-and-fro motion[cite: 43, 541].

concepts.lesson: Sound travels through a substance called a medium, which can be a solid, liquid, or gas[cite: 51, 52, 549, 550]. As an object vibrates, it sets nearby particles in motion[cite: 53, 551]. These particles don't travel to the ear themselves; instead, they displace adjacent particles and return to their original position, passing the disturbance forward[cite: 54, 56, 58, 552, 554, 556].
concepts.real_example: Air is the most common medium for sound travel[cite: 65, 563].
key_points: Sound requires a medium to travel[cite: 51, 549].
It is the disturbance, not the particles, that travels[cite: 60, 62, 558, 560].
Sound waves are mechanical waves[cite: 64, 562].

concepts.lesson: When a vibrating object moves forward, it pushes and compresses the air, creating a high-pressure region called a compression (C)[cite: 66, 67, 564, 565]. When it moves backward, it creates a low-pressure region called a rarefaction (R)[cite: 69, 567]. A series of these pressure variations forms a sound wave[cite: 70, 71, 568, 569].
concepts.real_example: A vibrating tuning fork creating pressure waves in the air[cite: 76, 574].
key_points: Compression is a region of high pressure and density[cite: 79, 81, 577, 579].
Rarefaction is a region of low pressure and density[cite: 79, 81, 577, 579].
Propagation is the movement of these density variations[cite: 82, 580].

concepts.lesson: Sound waves are longitudinal waves[cite: 107, 605]. This means the particles of the medium move back and forth in a direction parallel to the direction the wave is traveling[cite: 105, 603]. This is different from transverse waves, where particles move up and down perpendicular to the wave direction[cite: 110, 608].
concepts.real_example: A wave moving through a stretched slinky when it is pushed and pulled[cite: 101, 599].
key_points: Particles move parallel to the wave direction[cite: 105, 603].
Sound waves are categorized as longitudinal[cite: 107, 605].
Light is a transverse wave, not a mechanical one[cite: 112, 610].

concepts.lesson: Wavelength (λ) is the distance between two consecutive compressions or rarefactions[cite: 126, 624]. Frequency (v) is the number of complete oscillations per unit time[cite: 172, 670]. Wave speed is calculated as frequency multiplied by wavelength ($V = λv$)[cite: 233, 234, 731, 732].
concepts.real_example: A sound with a frequency of 2 kHz and a wavelength of 35 cm travels at 700 m/s[cite: 235, 242, 733, 740].
key_points: The SI unit of wavelength is the metre (m)[cite: 127, 625].
The SI unit of frequency is the hertz (Hz)[cite: 133, 174, 631, 672].
Frequency is the reciprocal of the time period ($v = 1/T$)[cite: 179, 677].

concepts.lesson: Amplitude is the maximum disturbance of the medium from its mean value[cite: 188, 686]. The loudness of a sound is determined by its amplitude[cite: 199, 697]. A sound with higher energy has a larger amplitude and is heard as louder[cite: 200, 217, 698, 715].
concepts.real_example: Striking a table harder produces a louder sound because of a larger amplitude[cite: 215, 713].
key_points: Amplitude determines loudness or softness[cite: 199, 697].
Loudness is the ear's response to sound intensity[cite: 253, 751].
Intensity is the sound energy passing through a unit area per second[cite: 251, 749].

concepts.lesson: Sound bounces off solid or liquid surfaces just like light[cite: 275, 276, 773, 774]. An echo is the reflected sound heard shortly after the original[cite: 299, 797]. To hear a distinct echo, the reflection must arrive at least 0.1 seconds after the original sound[cite: 301, 799].
concepts.real_example: Hearing your own voice back after shouting near a tall building or mountain[cite: 299, 797].
key_points: Sound follows the same laws of reflection as light[cite: 276, 774].
Minimum distance for a distinct echo in air at 22°C is 17.2 m[cite: 304, 802].
Successive reflections cause the rolling of thunder[cite: 306, 804].

concepts.lesson: In a large hall, sound persists due to repeated reflections from the walls[cite: 308, 806]. This persistence is called reverberation[cite: 309, 807]. Excessive reverberation can be reduced by using sound-absorbent materials like compressed fibreboard or heavy drapes on walls and ceilings[cite: 311, 809].
concepts.real_example: The design of auditoriums and cinemas to control sound persistence[cite: 311, 809].
key_points: Reverberation is the repeated reflection resulting in sound persistence[cite: 309, 807].
Undesirable in large halls if it is excessive[cite: 310, 808].
Absorbent materials are used to reduce it[cite: 311, 809].

concepts.lesson: The audible range for humans is roughly 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz[cite: 347, 845]. Sounds below 20 Hz are called infrasound (or infrasonic), and sounds above 20,000 Hz (20 kHz) are called ultrasound (or ultrasonic)[cite: 350, 355, 848, 853].
concepts.real_example: Elephants use infrasound to communicate, while bats use ultrasound to navigate[cite: 352, 356, 850, 854].
key_points: Human audible range: 20 Hz – 20 kHz[cite: 347, 845].
Infrasound is below 20 Hz[cite: 350, 848].
Ultrasound is above 20 kHz[cite: 355, 853].

concepts.lesson: Ultrasound has high frequency and can travel through obstacles along well-defined paths[cite: 372, 870]. It is used for cleaning hard-to-reach parts, detecting flaws in metal structures, and medical imaging[cite: 374, 377, 389, 872, 875, 887].
concepts.real_example: Echocardiography uses ultrasound to create images of the heart[cite: 388, 389, 886, 887].
key_points: Used to detect cracks in metal blocks[cite: 377, 875].
Ultrasound scanners image internal organs like the liver or kidneys[cite: 389, 390, 887, 888].
Can be used to break kidney stones into fine grains[cite: 437, 935].
